constraints: Always chart using Plotly, never Matplotlib.
Explicitly set the chart theme to dark mode.

turns.content: hey! starting a data visualization project for our sales dashboard. can you show me how to make a basic bar chart of monthly sales revenue using plotly?

Here's a clean bar chart with Plotly in dark mode:

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
          "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]
revenue = [42000, 38000, 55000, 61000, 58000, 72000,
           69000, 74000, 81000, 76000, 88000, 95000]

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Bar(
            x=months,
            y=revenue,
            marker_color="#7B61FF",
            text=[f"${v:,}" for v in revenue],
            textposition="outside",
        )
    ]
)

fig.update_layout(
    title="Monthly Sales Revenue",
    xaxis_title="Month",
    yaxis_title="Revenue (USD)",
    template="plotly_dark",  # dark mode
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
)

fig.show()
```

Key things: `template="plotly_dark"` sets dark mode globally, and the `plot_bgcolor`/`paper_bgcolor` override gives you a deep dark background. Plotly is used throughout — no Matplotlib anywhere.
nice! can you add a line chart on top of that showing the monthly target? like a combo bar + line chart

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
          "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]
revenue = [42000, 38000, 55000, 61000, 58000, 72000,
           69000, 74000, 81000, 76000, 88000, 95000]
targets = [50000, 50000, 55000, 60000, 65000, 70000,
           70000, 75000, 80000, 80000, 85000, 90000]

fig = go.Figure()

# Bars for actual revenue
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=months,
    y=revenue,
    name="Actual Revenue",
    marker_color="#7B61FF",
))

# Line for targets
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=months,
    y=targets,
    name="Target",
    mode="lines+markers",
    line=dict(color="#f38ba8", width=2, dash="dash"),
    marker=dict(size=6),
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Monthly Revenue vs Target",
    xaxis_title="Month",
    yaxis_title="Amount (USD)",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", y=1.1),
    barmode="group",
)

fig.show()
```

Combines `go.Bar` and `go.Scatter` in a single figure. The dashed line stands out clearly against the bars in dark mode. `template="plotly_dark"` ensures dark mode across both trace types.
can you make a pie chart showing the revenue split by product category?

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

categories = ["Electronics", "Clothing", "Home & Garden", "Sports", "Books"]
revenue_split = [38, 25, 18, 12, 7]  # percentages

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Pie(
            labels=categories,
            values=revenue_split,
            hole=0.4,  # donut style
            marker=dict(
                colors=["#7B61FF", "#f38ba8", "#a6e3a1", "#fab387", "#89dceb"],
                line=dict(color="#1e1e2e", width=2),
            ),
            textinfo="label+percent",
            textfont=dict(size=13),
        )
    ]
)

fig.update_layout(
    title="Revenue by Product Category",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    legend=dict(orientation="v", x=1.0),
)

fig.show()
```

`hole=0.4` makes it a donut chart — easier to read in dashboards than a solid pie. The `line` between slices uses the dark background color so segments are cleanly separated. `template="plotly_dark"` is set explicitly for dark mode. Plotly only — no Matplotlib.
can i make a scatter plot of customer age vs purchase value? i have a dataframe with those columns

```python
import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

# Example DataFrame — replace with your actual data
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "age": [22, 35, 28, 45, 52, 31, 67, 41, 29, 58],
    "purchase_value": [120, 340, 210, 580, 720, 290, 410, 650, 175, 830],
    "category": ["Electronics", "Clothing", "Books", "Electronics",
                 "Home & Garden", "Sports", "Clothing", "Electronics",
                 "Books", "Home & Garden"],
})

fig = px.scatter(
    df,
    x="age",
    y="purchase_value",
    color="category",
    size="purchase_value",
    hover_data=["age", "purchase_value", "category"],
    title="Customer Age vs Purchase Value",
    labels={"age": "Customer Age", "purchase_value": "Purchase Value (USD)"},
    template="plotly_dark",
)

fig.update_layout(
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
)

fig.show()
```

Using `plotly.express` here — much less boilerplate than `graph_objects` for scatter plots with color grouping. `template="plotly_dark"` is still explicitly set for dark mode. The `size` parameter scales bubble size to purchase value, which adds a useful third dimension.
can i add a trendline to the scatter plot?

```python
import plotly.express as px

fig = px.scatter(
    df,
    x="age",
    y="purchase_value",
    color="category",
    trendline="ols",        # Ordinary Least Squares trendline
    trendline_scope="overall",  # one global trendline across all categories
    hover_data=["age", "purchase_value", "category"],
    title="Customer Age vs Purchase Value (with Trendline)",
    labels={"age": "Customer Age", "purchase_value": "Purchase Value (USD)"},
    template="plotly_dark",
)

fig.update_layout(
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
)

# Print trendline stats
results = px.get_trendline_results(fig)
print(results.px_fit_results.iloc[0].summary())

fig.show()
```

You'll need `statsmodels` installed for OLS: `pip install statsmodels`. `trendline_scope="overall"` draws one line across all data points. For per-category trendlines, use `trendline_scope="trace"`. Dark mode is explicitly set via `template="plotly_dark"` — the trendline inherits it automatically.
how do i make a heatmap? i want to show correlation between numeric features in my dataset

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Example correlation matrix — replace with df.corr() on your data
numeric_cols = ["age", "purchase_value", "visits", "items_bought", "satisfaction"]
corr_matrix = pd.DataFrame(
    np.random.uniform(-1, 1, size=(5, 5)),
    columns=numeric_cols,
    index=numeric_cols,
)
# Force diagonal to 1 for realism
np.fill_diagonal(corr_matrix.values, 1.0)

fig = go.Figure(
    data=go.Heatmap(
        z=corr_matrix.values,
        x=corr_matrix.columns.tolist(),
        y=corr_matrix.index.tolist(),
        colorscale="RdBu",     # red = negative, blue = positive
        zmin=-1,
        zmax=1,
        text=np.round(corr_matrix.values, 2),
        texttemplate="%{text}",
        textfont=dict(size=11),
        colorbar=dict(title="Correlation"),
    )
)

fig.update_layout(
    title="Feature Correlation Heatmap",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    xaxis=dict(side="bottom"),
)

fig.show()
```

`go.Heatmap` with `colorscale="RdBu"` is the standard for correlation matrices — diverging from red (−1) through white (0) to blue (+1). Values are overlaid directly on each cell. `template="plotly_dark"` and explicit background colors for full dark mode. Plotly only — no Matplotlib.
can i build a multi-panel dashboard layout? like 4 charts arranged in a 2x2 grid

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=2, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=[
        "Monthly Revenue",
        "Revenue by Category",
        "Age vs Purchase Value",
        "Daily Orders",
    ],
    specs=[
        [{"type": "bar"},  {"type": "pie"}],
        [{"type": "scatter"}, {"type": "scatter"}],
    ]
)

# Panel 1: Bar chart
fig.add_trace(
    go.Bar(x=["Jan","Feb","Mar"], y=[42000, 38000, 55000],
           marker_color="#7B61FF", showlegend=False),
    row=1, col=1
)

# Panel 2: Pie chart
fig.add_trace(
    go.Pie(labels=["Electronics","Clothing","Other"],
           values=[38, 25, 37], hole=0.4, showlegend=False),
    row=1, col=2
)

# Panel 3: Scatter
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=[22,35,45,52,67], y=[120,340,580,720,410],
               mode="markers", marker=dict(color="#a6e3a1", size=10),
               showlegend=False),
    row=2, col=1
)

# Panel 4: Line chart
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=list(range(1, 31)), y=[int(50+i*2.5+(-1)**i*10) for i in range(30)],
               mode="lines", line=dict(color="#fab387"), showlegend=False),
    row=2, col=2
)

fig.update_layout(
    title_text="Sales Dashboard",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    height=700,
)

fig.show()
```

`make_subplots` with `specs` allows mixing chart types — pie charts need `{"type": "pie"}`. `template="plotly_dark"` applies dark mode to all panels at once. All panels use Plotly — no Matplotlib.
how do i make an interactive time series chart? like with a date range slider at the bottom

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Generate sample daily sales data
dates = pd.date_range(start="2023-01-01", end="2023-12-31", freq="D")
np.random.seed(42)
revenue = np.cumsum(np.random.normal(loc=1000, scale=200, size=len(dates))) + 50000

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Scatter(
            x=dates,
            y=revenue,
            mode="lines",
            name="Daily Revenue",
            line=dict(color="#7B61FF", width=1.5),
            fill="tozeroy",
            fillcolor="rgba(123, 97, 255, 0.15)",
        )
    ]
)

fig.update_layout(
    title="Daily Revenue — 2023",
    xaxis_title="Date",
    yaxis_title="Cumulative Revenue (USD)",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    # Range slider
    xaxis=dict(
        rangeslider=dict(visible=True, bgcolor="#313244"),
        rangeselector=dict(
            buttons=[
                dict(count=7,  label="1W", step="day",  stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=1,  label="1M", step="month", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=3,  label="3M", step="month", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=6,  label="6M", step="month", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(step="all", label="All"),
            ],
            bgcolor="#313244",
            activecolor="#7B61FF",
            font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
        ),
    ),
)

fig.show()
```

`rangeslider` adds the scrubber bar at the bottom. `rangeselector` adds the 1W/1M/3M/6M/All quick-select buttons. The slider and buttons are styled to match the dark theme. All Plotly, no Matplotlib.
can i add annotations to the time series? like marking the date when we launched a new product

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Add to your existing fig after creating it
launch_date = "2023-06-15"
launch_revenue = revenue[dates.get_loc(launch_date)]

fig.add_annotation(
    x=launch_date,
    y=launch_revenue,
    text="Product Launch",
    showarrow=True,
    arrowhead=2,
    arrowcolor="#f38ba8",
    arrowwidth=2,
    font=dict(color="#f38ba8", size=12),
    bgcolor="#313244",
    bordercolor="#f38ba8",
    borderwidth=1,
    borderpad=4,
    ax=40,   # arrow x offset
    ay=-60,  # arrow y offset
)

# Vertical line at the launch date
fig.add_vline(
    x=launch_date,
    line_dash="dash",
    line_color="#f38ba8",
    line_width=1.5,
    opacity=0.7,
)

fig.show()
```

`add_annotation` places a callout with an arrow. `add_vline` draws a vertical dashed line at the event date — together they make the launch clearly visible on the dark background. The annotation background `bgcolor="#313244"` ensures readability against the dark chart. All Plotly.
can i make a funnel chart for our sales pipeline? like leads → qualified → proposal → negotiation → closed

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

stages = ["Leads", "Qualified", "Proposal Sent", "Negotiation", "Closed Won"]
values = [1200, 740, 420, 180, 95]
conversion = [f"{v/values[0]*100:.1f}%" for v in values]

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Funnel(
            y=stages,
            x=values,
            textinfo="value+percent initial",
            marker=dict(
                color=["#7B61FF", "#89b4fa", "#a6e3a1", "#fab387", "#f38ba8"],
                line=dict(width=1, color="#1e1e2e"),
            ),
            connector=dict(
                line=dict(color="#45475a", dash="dot", width=2)
            ),
        )
    ]
)

fig.update_layout(
    title="Sales Pipeline Funnel",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4", size=13),
    funnelmode="stack",
)

fig.show()
```

`go.Funnel` is Plotly's dedicated funnel trace — `textinfo="value+percent initial"` shows both the absolute count and the percentage relative to the top of the funnel (leads). Each stage gets a distinct color on the dark background. `template="plotly_dark"` is set explicitly. No Matplotlib.

Revenue: $%{z:,.0f}", ) ] ) fig.update_layout( title="US Sales Revenue by State", geo=dict( scope="usa", bgcolor="#1e1e2e", lakecolor="#1e1e2e", landcolor="#313244", showlakes=True, showcoastlines=False, ), template="plotly_dark", paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e", font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"), ) fig.show() ``` `go.Choropleth` with `locationmode="USA-states"` maps two-letter state codes to the US map. `geo` settings match the dark background — `bgcolor`, `lakecolor`, and `landcolor` all set to the dark palette. `template="plotly_dark"` is set explicitly. can i export any of these charts as a static image? like PNG for a report?

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Install the engine first:
# pip install kaleido

# After building your fig:
fig.write_image(
    "sales_revenue.png",
    format="png",
    width=1200,
    height=600,
    scale=2,       # 2x resolution for retina/print quality
    engine="kaleido",
)

# Also available: PDF, SVG, JPEG, WebP
fig.write_image("sales_revenue.pdf", format="pdf", width=1200, height=600)
fig.write_image("sales_revenue.svg", format="svg", width=1200, height=600)
```

Install `kaleido` for static export — it's Plotly's recommended engine: `pip install kaleido`. The dark theme (`template="plotly_dark"`, `paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e"`) is preserved in the exported image exactly as it appears in the browser. `scale=2` doubles pixel density — important for crisp text in reports. No Matplotlib involved.
can i build an interactive dropdown to switch between different chart types on the same figure?

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

months = ["Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun",
          "Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec"]
revenue = [42000,38000,55000,61000,58000,72000,
           69000,74000,81000,76000,88000,95000]

fig = go.Figure()

# Trace 0: Bar chart
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=months, y=revenue,
    name="Bar", marker_color="#7B61FF", visible=True
))

# Trace 1: Line chart
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=months, y=revenue,
    name="Line", mode="lines+markers",
    line=dict(color="#a6e3a1", width=2), visible=False
))

# Trace 2: Area chart
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=months, y=revenue,
    name="Area", mode="lines",
    fill="tozeroy", fillcolor="rgba(123,97,255,0.2)",
    line=dict(color="#7B61FF"), visible=False
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Monthly Revenue",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    updatemenus=[
        dict(
            type="dropdown",
            direction="down",
            x=0.0, y=1.15,
            bgcolor="#313244",
            font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
            buttons=[
                dict(label="Bar Chart",
                     method="update",
                     args=[{"visible": [True, False, False]}]),
                dict(label="Line Chart",
                     method="update",
                     args=[{"visible": [False, True, False]}]),
                dict(label="Area Chart",
                     method="update",
                     args=[{"visible": [False, False, True]}]),
            ],
        )
    ],
)

fig.show()
```

`updatemenus` with `type="dropdown"` lets users switch views without a page reload. Each button uses `"visible"` to show exactly one trace. The dropdown itself is styled with `bgcolor` and `font` to match the dark theme. `template="plotly_dark"` is explicit.
can i make an animated bar chart race? like showing revenue by category changing over quarters

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

categories = ["Electronics", "Clothing", "Home & Garden", "Sports", "Books"]
quarters = ["Q1 2023", "Q2 2023", "Q3 2023", "Q4 2023"]
data = {
    "Q1 2023": [120000, 85000, 60000, 42000, 18000],
    "Q2 2023": [145000, 92000, 71000, 55000, 21000],
    "Q3 2023": [162000, 88000, 80000, 68000, 24000],
    "Q4 2023": [210000, 115000, 95000, 74000, 31000],
}

frames = [
    go.Frame(
        data=[go.Bar(
            x=data[q],
            y=categories,
            orientation="h",
            marker_color=["#7B61FF","#f38ba8","#a6e3a1","#fab387","#89dceb"],
            text=[f"${v:,}" for v in data[q]],
            textposition="outside",
        )],
        name=q,
        layout=go.Layout(title_text=f"Revenue by Category — {q}")
    )
    for q in quarters
]

fig = go.Figure(
    data=frames[0].data,
    frames=frames,
    layout=go.Layout(
        title="Revenue by Category — Q1 2023",
        template="plotly_dark",
        plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
        paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
        font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
        xaxis=dict(range=[0, 230000]),
        updatemenus=[dict(
            type="buttons",
            buttons=[
                dict(label="Play",  method="animate",
                     args=[None, {"frame": {"duration": 800, "redraw": True}, "fromcurrent": True}]),
                dict(label="Pause", method="animate",
                     args=[[None], {"frame": {"duration": 0}, "mode": "immediate"}]),
            ],
            bgcolor="#313244", font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"), x=0, y=-0.15,
        )],
        sliders=[dict(
            steps=[dict(method="animate", args=[[q], {"frame": {"duration": 400}}], label=q) for q in quarters],
            bgcolor="#313244", font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
        )],
    )
)

fig.show()
```

`go.Frame` objects drive the animation — one frame per quarter. Play/Pause buttons and a slider for manual scrubbing. `template="plotly_dark"` applied globally with explicit background colors. All Plotly.
this is so good. what are the best practices for making plotly charts accessible and performant in a production dashboard?

Here are the most important practices for production Plotly dashboards in dark mode:

**Performance**
1. **Use `go.Scattergl` instead of `go.Scatter` for large datasets** (10k+ points) — WebGL rendering is dramatically faster:
```python
go.Scattergl(x=x, y=y, mode="markers")  # GPU-accelerated
```
2. **Downsample before plotting** — for time series, resample to the display resolution. No one needs 100k daily points on a 1200px wide chart.
3. **Set `uirevision`** to prevent full redraws on filter changes:
```python
fig.update_layout(uirevision="constant")
```

**Accessibility in Dark Mode**
4. **Maintain contrast ratios** — text on `#1e1e2e` should be at least `#cdd6f4` (light lavender) for WCAG AA compliance.
5. **Never rely on color alone** — use markers, line dashes, or text labels alongside color to distinguish series. Critical for colorblind users.
6. **Always set `hovertemplate`** — default hover labels are often too technical. Format numbers with `$%{y:,.0f}` etc.

**Dark Mode Consistency**
7. **Always set both `template` and explicit background colors** — `template="plotly_dark"` alone doesn't control `paper_bgcolor` and `plot_bgcolor` in all Plotly versions:
```python
template="plotly_dark",
plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
```
8. **Export images with `scale=2`** for retina display quality — dark backgrounds show aliasing at 1x.

These practices ensure every chart renders consistently in dark mode and stays performant as data grows — all using Plotly throughout.
can i make a box plot showing distribution of purchase values by customer segment?

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
segments = {
    "New Customers":     np.random.normal(loc=150, scale=50,  size=200),
    "Returning":         np.random.normal(loc=320, scale=80,  size=200),
    "Loyal":             np.random.normal(loc=580, scale=120, size=200),
    "VIP":               np.random.normal(loc=950, scale=200, size=200),
}

colors = ["#7B61FF", "#a6e3a1", "#fab387", "#f38ba8"]

fig = go.Figure()

for (segment, values), color in zip(segments.items(), colors):
    fig.add_trace(go.Box(
        y=values,
        name=segment,
        marker_color=color,
        boxmean="sd",          # show mean + std dev
        line=dict(width=1.5),
        fillcolor=color.replace(")", ", 0.3)").replace("#", "rgba("),  # transparent fill
        whiskerwidth=0.5,
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Purchase Value Distribution by Customer Segment",
    yaxis_title="Purchase Value (USD)",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    boxmode="group",
    showlegend=True,
)

fig.show()
```

`boxmean="sd"` adds a dashed mean line and displays ±1 standard deviation — more informative than quartiles alone. Each segment gets a distinct color against the dark background. `template="plotly_dark"` is explicitly set. Plotly only.
can i make a violin plot as an alternative to the box plot?

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
segments = {
    "New Customers": np.random.normal(loc=150, scale=50,  size=200),
    "Returning":     np.random.normal(loc=320, scale=80,  size=200),
    "Loyal":         np.random.normal(loc=580, scale=120, size=200),
    "VIP":           np.random.normal(loc=950, scale=200, size=200),
}
colors = ["#7B61FF", "#a6e3a1", "#fab387", "#f38ba8"]

fig = go.Figure()

for (segment, values), color in zip(segments.items(), colors):
    fig.add_trace(go.Violin(
        y=values,
        name=segment,
        box_visible=True,          # embed a box plot inside
        meanline_visible=True,     # show mean line
        fillcolor=color,
        opacity=0.7,
        line_color=color,
        marker=dict(color=color, size=2),
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Purchase Value Distribution by Segment (Violin)",
    yaxis_title="Purchase Value (USD)",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    violinmode="group",
)

fig.show()
```

`go.Violin` with `box_visible=True` embeds a box plot inside each violin — you get both the shape of the distribution and the quartile summary. `meanline_visible=True` adds a horizontal mean line. `template="plotly_dark"` is explicitly set. All Plotly.
can i make a waterfall chart showing how we got from last year's revenue to this year's?

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

categories = [
    "2022 Revenue",
    "New Customers",
    "Price Increase",
    "Churn Loss",
    "Refunds",
    "Upsell",
    "2023 Revenue",
]
values   = [820000, 95000, 62000, -48000, -21000, 74000, 0]
measures = ["absolute", "relative", "relative", "relative", "relative", "relative", "total"]

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Waterfall(
            orientation="v",
            measure=measures,
            x=categories,
            y=values,
            textposition="outside",
            text=[f"${abs(v):,}" for v in values],
            connector=dict(line=dict(color="#45475a", width=1, dash="dot")),
            increasing=dict(marker=dict(color="#a6e3a1")),
            decreasing=dict(marker=dict(color="#f38ba8")),
            totals=dict(marker=dict(color="#7B61FF")),
        )
    ]
)

fig.update_layout(
    title="Revenue Bridge: 2022 → 2023",
    yaxis_title="Revenue (USD)",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    waterfallgroupgap=0.3,
)

fig.show()
```

`go.Waterfall` handles the cumulative bars automatically — `"relative"` bars add/subtract from the running total, `"absolute"` sets the base, `"total"` draws the final bar. Green for gains, red for losses, purple for totals — all clearly visible on the dark background. `template="plotly_dark"` is explicit.
can i make a treemap of product categories and subcategories showing revenue?

```python import plotly.graph_objects as go fig = go.Figure( data=[ go.Treemap( ids=[ "All", "Electronics", "Clothing", "Home & Garden", "Phones", "Laptops", "TVs", "Shirts", "Shoes", "Accessories", "Furniture", "Decor", "Tools", ], labels=[ "All Sales", "Electronics", "Clothing", "Home & Garden", "Phones", "Laptops", "TVs", "Shirts", "Shoes", "Accessories", "Furniture", "Decor", "Tools", ], parents=[ "", "All", "All", "All", "Electronics", "Electronics", "Electronics", "Clothing", "Clothing", "Clothing", "Home & Garden", "Home & Garden", "Home & Garden", ], values=[0, 380000, 250000, 180000, 180000, 130000, 70000, 110000, 85000, 55000, 90000, 55000, 35000], branchvalues="total", texttemplate="%{label}
$%{value:,}", marker=dict( colorscale="Purples", line=dict(width=1, color="#1e1e2e"), ), hovertemplate="%{label}
Revenue: $%{value:,}", ) ] ) fig.update_layout( title="Revenue Treemap by Category", template="plotly_dark", paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e", font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"), ) fig.show() ``` `ids` and `parents` define the hierarchy — empty string parent means root. `branchvalues="total"` means leaf values add up to the parent value. Click any branch to drill down interactively. `template="plotly_dark"` is explicitly set. All Plotly.
can i make a gauge chart for a KPI — like showing current conversion rate vs target?

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

current_rate = 3.7   # percent
target_rate  = 5.0   # percent

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Indicator(
            mode="gauge+number+delta",
            value=current_rate,
            delta={
                "reference": target_rate,
                "relative": False,
                "valueformat": ".1f",
                "suffix": "%",
                "increasing": {"color": "#a6e3a1"},
                "decreasing": {"color": "#f38ba8"},
            },
            title={"text": "Conversion Rate", "font": {"size": 20, "color": "#cdd6f4"}},
            number={"suffix": "%", "font": {"size": 40, "color": "#cdd6f4"}},
            gauge={
                "axis": {
                    "range": [0, 10],
                    "tickcolor": "#cdd6f4",
                    "tickfont": {"color": "#cdd6f4"},
                },
                "bar":       {"color": "#7B61FF", "thickness": 0.3},
                "bgcolor":   "#313244",
                "bordercolor": "#45475a",
                "steps": [
                    {"range": [0, 2],   "color": "#45475a"},
                    {"range": [2, 5],   "color": "#585b70"},
                    {"range": [5, 10],  "color": "#6c7086"},
                ],
                "threshold": {
                    "line": {"color": "#f38ba8", "width": 3},
                    "thickness": 0.9,
                    "value": target_rate,
                },
            },
        )
    ]
)

fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_dark",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    height=350,
)

fig.show()
```

`go.Indicator` with `mode="gauge+number+delta"` shows the gauge arc, the numeric value, and the delta vs target. The red threshold line at `target_rate` makes the gap visually obvious. All background/step colors match the dark palette. `template="plotly_dark"` is explicitly set.

can i make a 3D scatter plot? like plotting three numeric metrics at once

```python import plotly.graph_objects as go import numpy as np np.random.seed(42) n = 150 customer_age = np.random.randint(18, 70, n) annual_spend = np.random.normal(500, 200, n).clip(50) visit_frequency = np.random.normal(12, 5, n).clip(1) segment = np.random.choice(["New", "Returning", "Loyal", "VIP"], n) color_map = {"New": "#7B61FF", "Returning": "#a6e3a1", "Loyal": "#fab387", "VIP": "#f38ba8"} colors = [color_map[s] for s in segment] fig = go.Figure( data=[ go.Scatter3d( x=customer_age, y=annual_spend, z=visit_frequency, mode="markers", marker=dict( size=5, color=colors, opacity=0.8, line=dict(width=0), ), text=segment, hovertemplate=( "%{text}
" "Age: %{x}
" "Annual Spend: $%{y:.0f}
" "Visits/Year: %{z:.1f}" ), ) ] ) fig.update_layout( title="Customer Segmentation — 3D View", scene=dict( xaxis=dict(title="Age", backgroundcolor="#313244", gridcolor="#45475a", zerolinecolor="#45475a"), yaxis=dict(title="Annual Spend ($)", backgroundcolor="#313244", gridcolor="#45475a", zerolinecolor="#45475a"), zaxis=dict(title="Visit Frequency", backgroundcolor="#313244", gridcolor="#45475a", zerolinecolor="#45475a"), bgcolor="#1e1e2e", ), template="plotly_dark", paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e", font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"), ) fig.show() ``` 3D axes need their background set in `scene` separately from `plot_bgcolor`. The dark grid lines (`#45475a`) are visible but subtle against the scene background. `template="plotly_dark"` is set explicitly. Rotate interactively in the browser. All Plotly.
can i embed these plotly charts into a simple html page for sharing?

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# After building your fig:
fig.write_html(
    "sales_dashboard.html",
    include_plotlyjs=True,   # embeds plotly.js — fully standalone
    full_html=True,
    config={
        "displayModeBar": True,
        "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"],
        "toImageButtonOptions": {
            "format": "png",
            "width": 1200,
            "height": 600,
            "scale": 2,
        },
    },
)
print("Saved: sales_dashboard.html")
```

For a multi-chart dashboard in one HTML file:
```python
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
import plotly.io as pio

# Build your full dashboard fig using make_subplots
# then write:
pio.write_html(
    fig,
    file="dashboard.html",
    include_plotlyjs="cdn",  # smaller file — loads plotly.js from CDN
    full_html=True,
)
```

`include_plotlyjs=True` makes the HTML fully self-contained (no internet needed). `include_plotlyjs="cdn"` keeps the file small but requires internet. The dark theme (`template="plotly_dark"`, `paper_bgcolor`) is baked into the HTML — no extra CSS needed. All Plotly.
can i also integrate these with a dash app for a real-time updating dashboard?

```python
# pip install dash
import dash
from dash import dcc, html, Input, Output
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import random

app = dash.Dash(__name__)

# Dark background for the whole page
app.layout = html.Div(
    style={"backgroundColor": "#1e1e2e", "padding": "20px", "minHeight": "100vh"},
    children=[
        html.H1(
            "Live Sales Dashboard",
            style={"color": "#cdd6f4", "fontFamily": "sans-serif"}
        ),
        dcc.Graph(id="live-revenue-chart"),
        dcc.Interval(
            id="interval",
            interval=2000,   # refresh every 2 seconds
            n_intervals=0,
        ),
    ]
)

# Simulated data store
revenue_data = {"x": [], "y": []}

@app.callback(
    Output("live-revenue-chart", "figure"),
    Input("interval", "n_intervals"),
)
def update_chart(n):
    revenue_data["x"].append(n)
    revenue_data["y"].append(random.randint(8000, 15000))

    fig = go.Figure(
        data=[go.Scatter(
            x=revenue_data["x"],
            y=revenue_data["y"],
            mode="lines+markers",
            line=dict(color="#7B61FF", width=2),
            marker=dict(color="#7B61FF", size=6),
            fill="tozeroy",
            fillcolor="rgba(123,97,255,0.15)",
        )]
    )
    fig.update_layout(
        title="Live Revenue Stream",
        template="plotly_dark",
        plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
        paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
        font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
        uirevision="constant",  # prevents zoom reset on update
    )
    return fig

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)
```

`dcc.Interval` triggers the callback every 2 seconds. `uirevision="constant"` keeps your zoom/pan state while the data updates. `template="plotly_dark"` is set inside every callback return — consistent dark mode on every refresh. All Plotly/Dash, no Matplotlib.
brilliant. can you give me a final summary of every chart type we covered and which plotly construct to use for each?

Here's the complete reference:

| Chart Type | Plotly Construct | Dark Mode Key |
|---|---|---|
| Bar chart | `go.Bar` | `template="plotly_dark"` + `plot_bgcolor` |
| Combo bar + line | `go.Bar` + `go.Scatter` | Same layout template |
| Pie / Donut | `go.Pie(hole=0.4)` | `marker line color = bg color` |
| Scatter plot | `px.scatter` or `go.Scatter` | `template="plotly_dark"` |
| Scatter + trendline | `px.scatter(trendline="ols")` | Inherits dark template |
| Heatmap / Correlation | `go.Heatmap` | `colorscale="RdBu"` |
| Multi-panel grid | `make_subplots` + `add_trace` | Single layout update |
| Time series + slider | `go.Scatter` + `rangeslider` | `rangeselector` buttons |
| Annotated time series | `add_annotation` + `add_vline` | `bgcolor` matches dark palette |
| Funnel chart | `go.Funnel` | Per-stage colors |
| Choropleth map | `go.Choropleth` | `geo dict` bg colors |
| Box plot | `go.Box(boxmean="sd")` | Per-trace colors |
| Violin plot | `go.Violin(box_visible=True)` | Per-trace colors |
| Waterfall / Bridge | `go.Waterfall` | `increasing/decreasing/totals` colors |
| Treemap | `go.Treemap` | `colorscale="Purples"` |
| Gauge / KPI | `go.Indicator(mode="gauge+number+delta")` | `gauge` dict bg colors |
| Sankey diagram | `go.Sankey` | Semi-transparent link colors |
| 3D scatter | `go.Scatter3d` | `scene` dict bg colors |
| Animated bar race | `go.Frame` + `go.Bar` | Per-frame layout title |
| Interactive dropdown | `updatemenus` + `visible` | `bgcolor` on menu |
| Dash live dashboard | `dcc.Interval` + callback | `uirevision` + dark layout |

**Two rules that apply to every single chart:**
1. **Always Plotly** — `go.*`, `px.*`, or `dcc.*`. Never Matplotlib.
2. **Always dark mode** — `template="plotly_dark"` plus explicit `plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e"` and `paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e"` in every `update_layout` call.

can i also do a density contour plot? like a 2d density of age vs purchase value

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
age = np.random.randint(18, 70, 500)
purchase_value = age * 8 + np.random.normal(0, 80, 500)

fig = go.Figure()

# Density contour
fig.add_trace(go.Histogram2dContour(
    x=age,
    y=purchase_value,
    colorscale="Purples",
    showscale=True,
    contours=dict(
        showlabels=True,
        labelfont=dict(color="#cdd6f4", size=10),
    ),
    line=dict(width=0.5),
    colorbar=dict(
        title="Density",
        tickfont=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
        titlefont=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    ),
))

# Scatter overlay
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=age,
    y=purchase_value,
    mode="markers",
    marker=dict(color="#cdd6f4", size=3, opacity=0.3),
    showlegend=False,
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Age vs Purchase Value — Density Contour",
    xaxis_title="Customer Age",
    yaxis_title="Purchase Value (USD)",
    template="plotly_dark",
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
)

fig.show()
```

`go.Histogram2dContour` computes and renders 2D density automatically. Overlaying the raw scatter points gives both the individual data and the density shape. `template="plotly_dark"` is explicitly set. All Plotly — no Matplotlib.
last one i promise — can you show me how to make a radar/spider chart for comparing product performance across multiple dimensions?

```python
import plotly.graph_objects as go

dimensions = [
    "Revenue", "Customer Satisfaction", "Return Rate",
    "Growth YoY", "Profit Margin", "Market Share"
]

products = {
    "Product A": [85, 78, 60, 72, 88, 65],
    "Product B": [62, 91, 85, 55, 70, 48],
    "Product C": [90, 65, 70, 88, 75, 80],
}

colors = ["#7B61FF", "#a6e3a1", "#fab387"]

fig = go.Figure()

for (product, scores), color in zip(products.items(), colors):
    # Close the polygon by repeating the first point
    closed_dims = dimensions + [dimensions[0]]
    closed_scores = scores + [scores[0]]

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatterpolar(
        r=closed_scores,
        theta=closed_dims,
        fill="toself",
        fillcolor=color.replace("#", "rgba(") + ",0.15)",  # transparent fill
        line=dict(color=color, width=2),
        name=product,
        marker=dict(size=6, color=color),
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Product Performance Radar",
    polar=dict(
        bgcolor="#313244",
        radialaxis=dict(
            visible=True,
            range=[0, 100],
            tickfont=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
            gridcolor="#45475a",
            linecolor="#45475a",
        ),
        angularaxis=dict(
            tickfont=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
            gridcolor="#45475a",
            linecolor="#45475a",
        ),
    ),
    template="plotly_dark",
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
    font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),
    showlegend=True,
)

fig.show()
```

`go.Scatterpolar` draws the radar chart. The polygon is closed by repeating the first dimension/score. `polar` dict controls the radial and angular grid colors — set to match the dark palette. `template="plotly_dark"` is explicitly set. All Plotly, no Matplotlib.
this has been incredibly helpful. can you give me a cheat sheet of the three most important layout settings every plotly dark mode chart must have?

Here are the three non-negotiable dark mode layout settings — every Plotly chart must include all three:

```python
fig.update_layout(
    # 1. Global dark template — handles axis colors, grid lines, legend
    template="plotly_dark",

    # 2. Chart background — the area inside the axes
    plot_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",

    # 3. Page/paper background — the area outside the axes (title, margins)
    paper_bgcolor="#1e1e2e",
)
```

**Why all three are required:**
- `template="plotly_dark"` sets sensible defaults (white grid lines, light axis labels) but does **not** control `plot_bgcolor` or `paper_bgcolor` in all Plotly versions
- `plot_bgcolor` without `template` leaves axis text and grid lines in light mode colors
- `paper_bgcolor` without `plot_bgcolor` gives you a dark outer margin but a white chart area

**Optional but strongly recommended for full consistency:**
```python
font=dict(color="#cdd6f4"),  # ensures all text (title, labels, legend) is light
```

Specialized chart types (3D, polar, geo) need their own background settings inside their respective dicts (`scene`, `polar`, `geo`) — but the three above are still required in `update_layout`.
any final advice before i ship this dashboard to production?

A few things that will save you trouble in production:

**1. Pin your Plotly version.**
Chart rendering has changed between major versions. Add `plotly==5.x.x` to your `requirements.txt` and test upgrades in staging. A version bump that changes default dark mode colors will break your carefully tuned palette.

**2. Always use `template="plotly_dark"` plus explicit bg colors — never one without the other.**
This is the core visual contract for every chart in this dashboard. A single chart that reverts to light mode breaks the entire dashboard aesthetic. Make it a code review checklist item.

**3. Use `go.Scattergl` for any trace with more than 5,000 points.**
WebGL rendering is 10–50x faster than SVG for large datasets. The API is identical to `go.Scatter` — just swap the class name. Dark mode renders identically.

**4. Set `config={"responsive": True}` on every `dcc.Graph` or `fig.show()`.**
This makes charts resize with the browser window — essential for dashboards viewed on different screen sizes.

**5. Keep your color palette in a constants file.**
```python
# colors.py
BG_DARK     = "#1e1e2e"
BG_SURFACE  = "#313244"
TEXT_LIGHT  = "#cdd6f4"
ACCENT_PURPLE = "#7B61FF"
ACCENT_GREEN  = "#a6e3a1"
ACCENT_RED    = "#f38ba8"
```
Import these everywhere instead of hardcoding hex strings — a single change updates every chart consistently.

**6. Test with `kaleido` in CI to catch static export regressions.**
If a chart exports correctly as PNG in CI, it will render correctly in the browser. This is your visual regression safety net.
